subject: ap-chemistry
title: Thermochemistry & Calorimetry
stimulus:
A student performs an experiment to determine the value of the enthalpy change, DH , for the
rxn
oxidation-reduction reaction represented by the balanced equation above.

parts.prompt: Determine the oxidation number of Cl in NaOCl.
parts.scoring_notes: In NaOCl: Na is +1, O is −2. Sum of oxidation numbers = 0: (+1) + x + (−2) = 0 → x = +1. The oxidation number of Cl in NaOCl is +1.

scoring_points.description: For the correct oxidation state of Cl in NaOCl = +1: 1 point

required_elements: Oxidation number of Cl in NaOCl = +1.
alternatives.correct_example: In NaOCl: Na is +1 and O is −2. The compound is neutral: (+1) + (Cl) + (−2) = 0 → Cl = +1.

wrong_examples: Saying Cl = −1 — Cl is −1 in NaCl (no oxygen), but in NaOCl the oxygen is −2, making Cl = +1.
Saying Cl = +3 or +5 — those would require more than one O atom or a different compound.
common_traps: In hypochlorite (OCl$^-$): O is −2, so Cl must be +1 for the anion to have a −1 charge. Cl is oxidized to +1 here, NOT reduced.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the number of grams of Na
S
O
needed to prepare 100.00 mL of 0.500 M Na
S
O
(aq).
In the experiment, the student uses the solutions shown in the table below.
Concentration Volume
Solution
(M) (mL)
Na S O (aq) 0.500 5.00
2 2 3
NaOCl(aq) 0.500 5.00
NaOH(aq) 0.500 5.00

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using 5.00 mL (volume used in the experiment) instead of 100.00 mL (volume of the solution being prepared).
common_traps: Read carefully: the question asks for grams to prepare 100.00 mL of stock solution, not the volume used in the experiment (5.00 mL).

scoring_points.description: For the correct mass of Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ consistent with the number of moles: 1 point

required_elements: Mass = (0.0500 mol)(158.2 g/mol) = 7.91 g (accept values based on correct molar mass)

wrong_examples: Using the molar mass of NaCl or another compound instead of Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ = 158.2 g/mol.
common_traps: Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ molecular formula: 2 Na + 2 S + 3 O. Molar mass ≈ 158.1–158.2 g/mol (anhydrous).

parts.prompt:
Using the balanced equation for the oxidation-reduction reaction and the information in the table above,
determine which reactant is the limiting reactant. Justify your answer.
The solutions, all originally at 20.0°C, are combined in an insulated calorimeter. The temperature of the reaction
mixture is monitored, as shown in the graph below.
parts.scoring_notes: The balanced equation is S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$(aq) + 4 OCl$^-$(aq) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) → 2 SO$_4^{2-}$(aq) + 4 Cl$^-$(aq) + H$_2$O(l). Moles Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ = (0.500 M)(0.00500 L) = 0.00250 mol. Moles NaOCl = (0.500)(0.00500) = 0.00250 mol. Ratio needed: 1 S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ : 4 OCl$^-$. Available NaOCl only = 0.00250 mol but would need 4 × 0.00250 = 0.0100 mol for all S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$. NaOCl is the limiting reactant.

scoring_points.description: For identifying NaOCl as the limiting reactant with stoichiometric justification: 1 point

required_elements: From the balanced equation, the mole ratio S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ : OCl$^-$ = 1:4. With equal moles of each (0.00250 mol), NaOCl runs out first because it is needed in a 4:1 ratio relative to thiosulfate.
alternatives.correct_example: Moles Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ = Moles NaOCl = (0.500 M)(0.00500 L) = 0.00250 mol each. Balanced equation requires 1 mol S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$ per 4 mol OCl$^-$. To react with 0.00250 mol S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$, we need 0.0100 mol OCl$^-$, but only 0.00250 mol is available. NaOCl is the limiting reactant.

wrong_examples: Saying Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ is limiting — it is needed in only 1/4 the moles of OCl$^-$; it is actually in excess.
Comparing only moles without using the stoichiometric ratio — the 1:4 ratio must be applied.
common_traps: Limiting reactant determination: calculate the mole ratio from the balanced equation first. Equal volumes and equal concentrations do NOT mean equal moles of each reactant are needed.

parts.prompt: According to the graph, what is the temperature change of the reaction mixture?
parts.scoring_notes: From the graph: the temperature rises from 20.0°C (initial) to approximately 32.5°C (final, extrapolated back to time of mixing). ΔT = T$_f$ − T$_i$ = 32.5 − 20.0 = 12.5°C.

scoring_points.description: For the correct temperature change read from the graph: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: From the graph, the final temperature is approximately 32.5°C. $\Delta T = 32.5 - 20.0 = 12.5\text{ °C}$

wrong_examples: Reading the maximum temperature directly without subtracting the initial temperature.
Misreading the graph scale — the temperature axis must be read carefully.
common_traps: $\Delta T = T_f - T_i$. Read the extrapolated final temperature from the plateau, then subtract the initial temperature (20.0°C).

parts.prompt:
The mass of the reaction mixture inside the calorimeter is 15.21 g.
(i) Calculate the magnitude of the heat energy, in joules, that is released during the reaction. Assume that
the specific heat of the reaction mixture is 3.94 J/(g·°C) and that the heat absorbed by the calorimeter is
negligible.
(ii) Using the balanced equation for the oxidation-reduction reaction and your answer to part (c), calculate
the value of the enthalpy change of the reaction,
DH
, in kJ/mol . Include the appropriate algebraic
rxn rxn
sign with your answer.
The student repeats the experiment, but this time doubling the volume of each of the reactants, as shown in the
table below.
Concentration Volume
Solution
(M) (mL)
Na S O (aq) 0.500 10.0
2 2 3
NaOCl(aq) 0.500 10.0
NaOH(aq) 0.500 10.0
parts.scoring_notes: Part (e)(i): $q = mc\Delta T = (15.21\text{ g})(3.94\text{ J/(g·°C)})(12.5\text{ °C}) = 748.8\text{ J} \approx 749\text{ J}$. Part (e)(ii): Moles of limiting reactant (NaOCl) = 0.00250 mol. ΔH$_{rxn}$ = −q/n$_{limiting}$ per mole of rxn. From balanced equation, 4 mol OCl$^-$ consumed per mol rxn. Moles rxn = 0.00250/4 = 6.25 × 10$^{-4}$ mol. $\Delta H_{rxn} = -748.8\text{ J} / (6.25 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol}) = -1.198 \times 10^6\text{ J/mol} = -1200\text{ kJ/mol}$ (negative: exothermic).

scoring_points.description: For the correct heat released = $mc\Delta T$: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using mass of only one solution instead of the total mixture mass (15.21 g).
Using specific heat of water (4.18 J/(g·°C)) — the problem states 3.94 J/(g·°C).
common_traps: $q = mc\Delta T$ uses the total mass of the reaction mixture and the stated specific heat (3.94 J/(g·°C)).

scoring_points.description: For correct moles of reaction from the limiting reactant: 1 point

required_elements: Moles of NaOCl = 0.00250 mol; balanced equation uses 4 mol OCl$^-$ per 1 mol rxn.
alternatives.correct_example: From the balanced equation, 4 mol OCl$^-$ are consumed per mole of reaction. Moles rxn = 0.00250 mol OCl$^-$ / 4 = 6.25 × 10$^{-4}$ mol rxn.

wrong_examples: Dividing by 1 (1 mol thiosulfate per 1 mol rxn) — the reaction scale is set by the coefficient of the limiting reactant (OCl$^-$, coefficient = 4).
common_traps: ΔH$_{rxn}$ is per mole of reaction as written. Use the coefficient of the limiting reactant in the balanced equation to find moles of rxn.

scoring_points.description: For the correct negative sign and magnitude of ΔH$_{rxn}$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\Delta H_{rxn} = \frac{-748.8\text{ J}}{6.25 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol}} = -1.198 \times 10^6\text{ J/mol} = -1200\text{ kJ/mol}$
(negative because the temperature of the surroundings increased — heat is released)

wrong_examples: Reporting ΔH as positive — the temperature increased, so the reaction is exothermic (ΔH < 0).
Not converting J to kJ — divide by 1000.
common_traps: Exothermic reactions release heat to the surroundings → calorimeter temperature increases → ΔH$_{rxn}$ is negative. ΔH = −q$_{cal}$/n$_{rxn}$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct consistent answer with appropriate significant figures: 1 point

required_elements: Answer reported in kJ/mol with appropriate sig figs (3 sig figs: −1200 kJ/mol or −1.20 × 10$^3$ kJ/mol).
alternatives.correct_example: $\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -1.20 \times 10^3\text{ kJ/mol}_{rxn}$ (3 significant figures, based on ΔT = 12.5°C)

wrong_examples: Reporting in J/mol without converting to kJ — the answer should be in kJ/mol.
common_traps: ΔH$_{rxn}$ is reported per mole of reaction (as written in the balanced equation), not per mole of limiting reactant. Include units and sign.

parts.prompt:
The magnitude of the enthalpy change,
DH
, in kJ/mol , calculated from the results of the second
rxn rxn
experiment is the same as the result calculated in part (e)(ii). Explain this result.
parts.scoring_notes: By doubling the volumes (and doubling all moles), twice as much heat is released in the second experiment (q is doubled), but the moles of reaction are also doubled. Therefore ΔH$_{rxn}$ = −q/n$_{rxn}$ remains the same. Enthalpy change per mole of reaction (an intensive property) does not depend on the quantity of substance reacted.

scoring_points.description: For explaining that ΔH$_{rxn}$ is an intensive property (per mole) that is independent of the scale of the reaction: 1 point

required_elements: Doubling the volumes doubles both q and n$_{rxn}$.
ΔH$_{rxn}$ = −q/n$_{rxn}$ — numerator and denominator both double, so the ratio (ΔH$_{rxn}$) is unchanged.
alternatives.correct_example: In the second experiment, both the volume of each solution and the number of moles are doubled. Twice as much heat is produced ($q$ doubles), but twice as many moles react ($n_{rxn}$ doubles). Since $\Delta H_{rxn} = -q/n_{rxn}$, the ratio stays the same. ΔH$_{rxn}$ is an intensive quantity — it represents energy per mole of reaction, not the total energy released.

wrong_examples: Saying the same amount of heat is produced — twice the amount of reagents produces twice the heat; it is the ratio that is constant.
Saying ΔH changes with scale — ΔH$_{rxn}$ per mole of reaction is a state function independent of the scale.
common_traps: ΔH$_{rxn}$ is an intensive property (energy per mole). Scaling up reaction produces proportionally more heat and proportionally more moles. The per-mole ratio is constant.

parts.prompt:
Write the balanced net ionic equation for the given reaction.
2 NO(g) + $O_2$(g) → 2 NO (g)
2 2
parts.scoring_notes: The reaction of Na$_2$S$_2$O$_3$ with NaOCl in NaOH: the overall ionic equation is S$_2$O$_3^{2-}$(aq) + 4 OCl$^-$(aq) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) → 2 SO$_4^{2-}$(aq) + 4 Cl$^-$(aq) + H$_2$O(l). Spectator ions (Na$^+$) are omitted.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net ionic equation with spectator ions removed: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\text{S}_2\text{O}_3^{2-}(\text{aq}) + 4\text{ OCl}^-(\text{aq}) + 2\text{ OH}^-(\text{aq}) \rightarrow 2\text{ SO}_4^{2-}(\text{aq}) + 4\text{ Cl}^-(\text{aq}) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$
Na$^+$ is a spectator ion — omit it from the net ionic equation.

wrong_examples: Including Na$^+$ ions — these are spectators and must be cancelled from both sides.
Writing an unbalanced equation — check charges on both sides: left = −2 + 4(−1) + 2(−1) = −8; right = 2(−2) + 4(−1) + 0 = −8. ✓
common_traps: Net ionic equations omit spectator ions (ions that appear unchanged on both sides). Verify charge balance and atom balance separately.
